title: deferred playbook executor with scheduled dry-run and safe rollback hooks
preview: Motivation: On-call automation needs a way to schedule runbooks to execute outside peak windows, run a non-destructive dry-run before live execution, and ensure safe automatic rollback when automation makes state changes. This PR adds a Deferred Playbook Executor that accepts scheduled windows and optional dry-run payloads, a scheduled dry-run runner that simulates playbook steps without side effects, and pluggable safe-rollback hooks that capture pre-change snapshots and provide an automated revert path. Key design points: (1) All deferred executions are stored as immutable scheduling tasks with idempotency tokens, (2) dry-run mode routes actions to a simulator layer that returns predicted outcomes and a list of mutated resources, (3) rollback hooks register resource snapshotters and revert actions, and (4) RBAC/feature-flag gating prevents accidental live scheduling. This change also adds telemetry (metrics and logs) for scheduled_task.created, scheduled_task.executed, dry_run.result, and rollback.invoked, plus tracing tags for schedule_id and runbook_id. Rollout: feature-flag gated, initial rollout to staging for 2 weeks with synthetic scheduled dry-run tests, then canary to 10% of production namespaces. See linked Linear items for acceptance criteria and SLO checks.

title: Introduce randomized counterfactual probe and soft-fail CI adapter
preview: Adds a randomized counterfactual probe to the eval-harness and a soft-fail CI adapter that surfaces regressions without hard-blocking merges unless they exceed configurable statistical thresholds. Motivation: many regressions are brittle to minor prompt edits; counterfactual probing produces small, semantically preserving edits and measures sensitivity. Design: probe generator implements configurable perturbation strategies (entity swap, tense shift, negation flip, punctuation perturbation) with deterministic seeding for reproducibility. The CI adapter runs a small batched probe set against the PR head, computes a bootstrap-based p-value and effect-size estimate, and returns either OK, WARN, or FAIL based on thresholds. WARN is marked as a soft-failure that appears prominently in PR checks and in the console, encouraging fixes without stopping fast-moving teams. Includes CLI flags --counterfactual-seed, --cf-soft-fail-threshold, --cf-min-samples and an autoscaling backoff that reduces probe size when CI concurrency is high. Ties into existing eval telemetry to record sensitivity_score per-prompt and cohort, enabling later alerting and triage. Backwards compatibility: the adapter is opt-in via .eval-config or pipeline flag and defaults to disabled for release branches. Risk: false positives for very small cohorts are mitigated by bootstrap confidence intervals and a minimum-sample guard. Linked work: ENG-4217 for probe design and ENG-4218 for CI adapter integration. Usage: run `eh run --probe counterfactual --seed 1234` locally or rely on the GitHub workflow `counterfactual-smoke.yml` which is added in this PR.

title: Introduce timebox seeder and hierarchical telemetry compactifier for long-run benchmarks
preview:
Motivation: Long-duration benchmark runs in Benchmark Lab are noisy and expensive to store because we capture full-event traces and per-sample payloads for every iteration. Two common pain points: (1) realistic warm-cache steady-state is expensive to reach deterministically across different sequence-length mixes; (2) raw telemetry volume for long runs (multi-hour stress / soak) is large, which slows downstream analysis and inflates storage costs.

Design & changes: Introduce a timebox seeder (timebox-seeder) that drives a configurable, bounded priming stage prior to each measurement epoch. The seeder supports: target distributions (seq-length, token-priming prefixes, model-mix), controlled eviction pressure knobs, and deterministic seeding modes (seed schedules can be reused across runs for reproducibility). Parallel to the seeder, add a hierarchical telemetry compactifier (rollup-telemetry) which consumes sample-level events and emits multiresolution rollups: fixed-window histograms for latency/queueing, sparse top-k hotpath counts (kernel and code-path labels), and a compact KV-cache delta summary. Rollups are time-aligned to the benchmark epochs and include provenance tags so downstream reporters can reconstruct coarse timelines for debugging.

API & operator UX: New CLI targets: `benchctl seed --profile <yaml>` and `benchctl rollup --out <path>`. We also added two YAML profiles under bench/configs/profiles/ to demonstrate common web-chat and search-like mixes. The default CI benchmark workflow now runs a 2-minute timebox seed before each steady-state sampling window. The new rollup files are gzipped ndjson, with one record per rollup-epoch.

Results: On the representative multi-model chat workload (mix: 0.7 single-turn short, 0.3 multi-turn long) we observed: - telemetry footprint reduction: ~40x (raw events -> rollups) for a 2h soak; - steady-state p95 latency stability improved across repeated runs (stdev reduced by ~18%) because the timebox seeder produces consistent cache state; - wall-clock time to reach measurable steady-state reduced by 30% in our lab when using a 2-minute timebox profile vs no explicit seeding.

Backward compatibility: Rollup outputs are additive; we kept the raw capture path behind a flag so existing analysis pipelines continue to work. The seeder can be disabled per-run. No changes to existing benchmark schema were required.

Operational notes: Rollup writer includes a size threshold which auto-rotates files when the gzipped rollup file exceeds the configured limit (default 64MB). We added a lightweight sanity-checker binary that validates rollup provenance and TTL.

Why this is different from previous work: This PR focuses on two pragmatic operational bottlenecks: deterministic warm-cache priming in a timeboxed, reproducible way (as opposed to ad-hoc long warmups), and reducing telemetry storage/ingest costs for long soak/soak-like workloads while preserving actionable signals for regressions and hotspots.

Link to related Linear items: ENG-2384 (timebox seeding design), ENG-2401 (rollup format and storage savings).

title: Add benchmark serializer and release timeliner
preview:
Why: onboarding produces many per-variant benchmark artifacts (latency histograms, P50/P95 traces, throughput stamps). We need a compact, versioned on-disk format so the registry can store, index, and compose those artifacts into human-facing release notes and machine-checkable regression timelines. This PR introduces a small, portable 'benchmark serializer' and a 'release timeliner' service that composes micro-release entries into a chronological digest for changelogs and downgrade/rollout playbooks.

What: single PR that implements: (1) bench serializer (binary+JSON compact representation with v1 header and stable field ordering); (2) timeliner CLI and library that consumes serialized benchmark artifacts and emits a microrelease timeline (JSONL) and a condensed markdown digest for release notes; (3) CI hook to assemble timeline artifacts and attach a release digest draft to the linear ticket; (4) tests and docs.

Design notes: serializer is intentionally conservative: header = {format: "rdw.bench", version: "v1", model_id, variant, timestamp, schema_hash}. Payload stores compact arrays for histo buckets and numeric envelopes for p50/p90/p95/p99 and token-throughput samples. Strings are interned; field order is fixed to ensure deterministic hashes across languages. Timeliner groups artifacts by model and semantic version, applies heuristics to label regressions (>= 10% P95 regression and certainty heuristic based on sample counts), and emits per-release summary sentences plus a minimal YAML frontmatter so docs tooling can assemble it into release notes.

Checklist: [x] bench serializer implementation and roundtrip tests; [x] timeliner CLI + library; [x] deterministic hashing and stable ordering tests; [x] CI integration for release digest assembly; [x] docs and usage examples; [x] migration plan (no DB schema change; serializer stored as blobs under existing bench_artifacts table).

Commits summary: - 8d3f9b0: initial bench serializer and schema v1; - b4c12e1: add deterministic hashing and interning map; - c1a7f43: timeliner library + CLI + grouping heuristics; - ef2d4c9: add CI release hook and draft generator; - 3a7bce8: tests, docs, and examples.

Files touched (high-level): src/serializer/bench_serializer.py; src/timeliner/compile_timeline.py; cli/commands/timeliner.py; tests/test_bench_serializer.py; tests/test_timeliner_integration.py; docs/registry/release-timeliner.md; ci/release-timeliner-hook.yml; migrations/2025_02_add_bench_blob.sql; examples/benchmarks/sample_model_v1.bench.jsonl.

Review conversation (high level): 
- Diego: "This looks promising — two asks: ensure the serializer is language-portable (we may need a protobuf shim) and make the P95 regression threshold configurable via release policy."
- Ava: "Agree on portability. Kept v1 as a JSON-friendly compact blob; added a tiny proto shim in serializer/proto_shim.py and a schema_hash field so future versions can include protobuf encoding while keeping v1 header. Added --regression-threshold flag and policy config for timeliner."

- Priya: "Can we avoid blowing up storage? How big are typical serialized artifacts?"
- Ava: "Roundtrip tests show typical artifact ~1.2–3.4 KB once interned and compressed; CI stores gzipped blobs by default. We also added a retention hint and recommend registry retention policy for 'microrelease' artifacts (30/90/365)."

- Luca: "Please add more integration tests that simulate multiple variant arrivals and ensure timeline ordering is stable across clocks and ingestion races."
- Ava: "Added deterministic ordering using model_id+semver+timestamp and a tie-breaker of stable hash; added integration test 'test_timeliner_integration.py' that fuzzes arrival order."

CI notes: initial pipeline failed lint (black/flake8) and had a flaky test; fixed formatting and increased test timeout on CI; final run: all unit/integration tests passed, docs build succeeded, release hook validated.

Migration / Compatibility: no DB schema migrations needed. The serializer blobs are stored in the existing bench_artifacts table under new column 'artifact_blob' (added via non-disruptive migration script included but not required for storage backend that uses object store). Timeliner is opt-in: registry config key 'timeliner.enabled' defaults to false until feature roll is complete. v1 header allows future encodings; tooling will ignore unknown fields gracefully.

How to use (examples): 
- Serialize a benchmark: python -m src.serializer.bench_serializer --input samples/raw_latency.json --output sample_model_v2.bench
- Compile a timeline: redwood-registry timeliner compile --input-dir artifacts/ --output timeline-2025-02-17.jsonl --md-out release-digest-2025-02-17.md --regression-threshold 0.1

Why this is different from prior work: previous PRs focused on profile ingestion and release blurb generation in prose; this PR standardizes a compact, deterministic binary-friendly artifact format intended for long-lived storage and automated timeline assembly. It bridges low-level benchmark artifacts with user-facing release draft automation and enables machine-readable regression signals that can feed the alerting playbooks in SRE.

Merge: squash and merge after approvals. Merge message: "Add compact benchmark serializer (v1) + release timeliner; CLI, CI hook, tests, docs."

title: Add bounded-confidence replay CI hook and progressive quarantine
preview: Motivation: intermittent prompt-set regressions commonly triggered by sampling noise result in noisy CI failures and unnecessary quarantines. This change introduces a bounded-confidence replay CI hook that adaptively replays failing cohorts until a statistically sound confidence interval for the failure rate is obtained (Wilson score interval) or until a configured cap is reached. The hook also integrates a progressive-quarantine policy that transitions prompt cohorts through soft->hard quarantine states based on posterior confidence rather than raw failure counts. Design: ci/hooks/bounded_confidence_replay.py implements the replay loop, statistics and retry backoff; ci/policies/progressive_quarantine.yaml contains policy knobs (min_samples, max_replays, target_ci_width, soft_threshold, hard_threshold, decay). We expose a CLI toggle (--ci-bounded-replay) and a feature flag in CI config so rollout can be staged. Observability: replay-run provenance tags, counters for replays, per-cohort confidence width time-series, and a minimal Grafana dashboard are included. Testing: unit tests for statistical logic, integration test that simulates flaky prompts with seeded variability, and a GH Actions smoke matrix entry. Rollout plan: default disabled on main, enabled for eval-infra and canary repos for two weeks, then enabled org-wide if no regression signals. Checklist: [x] implementation + unit tests [x] integration smoke [x] metrics + dashboard [x] docs + runbook [x] Linear links added (ENG-2042).

title: add-frequency-domain-residuals-detector-and-graceful-threshold-adapter
preview: Motivation: transient, periodic interference (GC sweeps, noisy neighbors, periodic telemetry jobs) has caused low-amplitude latency regressions that percentile-only detectors occasionally miss and that can trigger noisy rollbacks. This PR introduces a new detector that analyzes latency time-series in the frequency domain and fuses spectral residual signals with existing percentile drift metrics to produce a more stable canary signal. Design summary: 1) Windowed FFT transform of per-request p95/p99 time series over short sliding windows (configurable); 2) Compute spectral residual energy in targeted bands (e.g., 0.5-5s cycles) and normalize by traffic power to be traffic-aware; 3) Produce a residuals_score time series and combine with percentile delta via a probabilistic fusion step (logistic weight based on signal SNR); 4) Graceful Threshold Adapter: thresholds are scaled by a lightweight traffic intensity estimator and by recent variance to avoid over-reacting to cold-start or low-sample windows; 5) Rollout guard: when residuals_score exceeds adaptive threshold for N consecutive windows we pause canary traffic and open an investigation alert with suggested probes. Implementation notes: detector implemented in perf-canary/src/detectors/freq_residuals.py using numpy/scipy primitives with a fallback optimized CFFI path for high-throughput hosts; fusion and threshold adapter live in perf-canary/src/eval/fusion.py; added PromQL metric exporters and Grafana panel JSON for visibility under observability/canaries/freq-residuals-panel.json. Tests and validation: unit tests cover spectral normalization, SNR-based weighting, and adapter scaling (perf-canary/tests/test_freq_residuals.py); integration canary harness added to perf-canary/integration/canary-sim that replays synthetic traffic with injected periodic noise to validate true/false-positive curves; load impact: CPU microbenchmarks show ~2% extra CPU on probe workers, latency overhead negligible. Rollout plan: feature flag behind perf.canary.freq_residuals.enabled; start with passive mode (score logged but not gating) for 48h in perf environment, promote to active gating on a small percentage of canaries, then global default after two weeks if false-positive rate < 1% and detection recall improves. Related work: builds on ENG-2480 (spectral baseline) and complements existing percentile-aware detectors in this codebase. Backwards compatibility: detector registers as optional evaluation plugin; default off for existing canaries. Metrics to watch: residuals_score, fused_canary_signal, false_positive_rate, mean_time-to-pause, CPU impact. Operational playbook: added runbook section pointing to runbooks/canaries/freq-residuals-runbook.md with RCA steps and suggested probes. This PR includes code, tests, panels, and runbook entries. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after approvals and green CI.

title: catalog: surface model capability flags, pricing and limits for OpenAI-compat clients
preview:
Motivation: Clients using the OpenAI-compat layer and our public model listing need a single, predictable surface to discover per-model capabilities (streaming stability, function/tool-calling support, structured-output specializations) and unit economics (pricing per token, soft/hard limits and concurrency). This PR extends the model catalog API and internal catalog objects to include capability and pricing metadata, plus server-side filters that power the console and SDKs.

Summary of changes: added explicit capability flags (openai_compat, supports_streaming_stable, supports_function_calling, structured_output_profiles) to the model listing; exposed priced_units and limits metadata (cost_per_input_token, cost_per_output_token, max_tokens_per_request, recommended_concurrency, rate_limit_tier). Implemented new query params for the GET /v1/models endpoint to filter by capability, max_estimated_cost_per_k, min_stability_score, region, and quantization support. Updated the OpenAI compatibility layer to prefer models with openai_compat=true for compatibility endpoints and to fall back predictably to compatible variants when a requested capability is not available. Added docs, SDK bindings, and tests.

Why this is safe: fields are additive. No breaking schema changes for existing consumers. Default behavior for compatibility endpoints is unchanged unless clients explicitly query the new fields or filtering. We included a migration path for older model entries missing cost metadata (defaults used and surfaced as "unknown").

API additions (examples):
- GET /v1/models?capability=function_calling&region=us-east-1&max_estimated_cost_per_k=0.50 -> returns models that advertise function calling and whose cost_per_1k_tokens <= 0.50.
- New model-listing response includes keys: capabilities (list), pricing (object), limits (object), compatibility_flags (list). Example snippet in response body (condensed for PR):

title: Introduce probabilistic span sampler and kernel branch-counter hooks for adaptive scheduler insights
preview:
Context and motivation: We see intermittent scheduler degradation for long-tail requests where KV cache hits are correlated with kernel branch behavior and micro-stalls. Existing NVTX and epoch counters capture timing but not branch-level dynamics. This PR introduces a probabilistic (weighted) span sampler that biases capture toward requests with high KV lookup latency and late-batch positions, plus a small set of kernel branch/mispredict counters exposed through the telemetry pipeline so dashboard panels can correlate scheduler decisions with low-level CPU/GPU branch behavior.

What this PR does (single-string description): Adds a lightweight probabilistic span sampler (runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.{cc,h}) that samples at enqueue time based on configurable weights: kv_hit_latency (ms), batch_index, and recent_retry_count. The sampler emits full NVTX-range + span payload for sampled requests only, keeping steady-state overhead near 0.3% at 95th percentile load in our microbench. Introduces kernel branch counters (telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp) that aggregate branch-mispredict and branch-instruction counts at the scheduler worker boundary; counters are aggregated per-epoch and batched to the existing telemetry pipeline. Updates perf-dashboard json (observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json) with panels that join span traces and branch counters, plus a dedicated query to highlight high-latency KV lookups with elevated branch-mispredict ratios. Includes unit tests and an integration microbench (bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc) that reproduces regression patterns.

Checklist (author): - [x] Implementation of weighted span sampler core - [x] Kernel branch counters and telemetry export - [x] Dashboard panels and query snippets - [x] Deterministic microbench and unit tests - [x] Docs for ops on how to interpret panels and sampler tuning

Changed areas (high-level file list): runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.cc, runtime/sampling/weighted_span_sampler.h, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.cpp, telemetry/kernel_branch_counters.h, observability/dashboards/weighted-span-sampler.json, bench/micro/sampler_adaptive_sched_bench.cc, tests/sampling/test_weighted_sampler.cpp, docs/observability/sampler.md, BUILD.bazel (telemetry exports).

Test/CI notes: CI performed three runs. First run failed a flaky perf-test (bench micro reliability) due to environmental jitter; second run re-ran the flaky job and passed. All unit tests and integration tests pass locally and in CI. End-to-end telemetry e2e smoke was validated in staging with a synthetic traffic pattern; dashboard queries returned expected joins.

Review conversation (selected highlights):
- Diego Martins (review): "Nice approach — weighting by KV hit latency makes sense. Two asks: (1) make the sampler weight function pluggable (so ops can try other heuristics), and (2) please add an opt-out runtime flag for customers concerned about any additional counters."
- Ava Chen (author reply): "Pluggable weight function is implemented via a SamplerStrategy interface (see sampling/strategy.h); default strategy remains the weighted heuristic. Added runtime flag --telemetry.disable_branch_counters with documentation and feature-gate check."
- Priya Patel (review): "Telemetry export looks good; can you add a dashboard note explaining expected baseline ranges for branch-mispredict ratio? Otherwise panels are noisy for new tenants."
- Ava Chen: "Added doc section with baseline guidance and a small script to compute baseline from a 1-hour sample."
- Marcus Lee (approval): "+1 after tests re-run — please squash on merge to keep history tidy."

Performance measurements: Microbench in bench/micro shows sampler overhead <0.5% p95 request CPU and memory delta in a 64-core staging cluster; sampling rate default is 0.1% (configurable). Branch counters add ~0.2% overhead at aggregation boundary when enabled. Combined telemetry ingestion size increased by ~0.8% at default sampling rate. Long-tail test: when enabling weighted sampling, traces for 99.9th percentile latency requests increased by ~18x (samples) enabling better RCA visibility.

Operational notes and config: Default config retains existing telemetry costs: sampler default rate 0.001 (0.1%), weight_exponent 1.2, kv_latency_threshold_ms 1.0. Operators can change strategy via telemetry.sampler.strategy=<name> and disable branch counters via telemetry.disable_branch_counters=true. Dashboard docs include PromQL/ESQL examples to compute mispredict_ratio = branch_mispredicts / branch_instructions.

Potential risks and mitigation: Branch counters require a small kernel shim for CPUs on some older distros — guarded behind feature gate and disabled by default. We instrument aggregation carefully to avoid contention (batch buffer + single flush per-epoch). If customers enable aggressive sampling, telemetry costs can increase; guidance added to docs.

Merge notes: Squash merge performed by Marcus Lee after CI re-run and approvals. Changelog entry added under Observability / Telemetry.

Related work and follow-ups: Follow-up tickets created to (1) explore GPU-side branch sampling equivalents (ENG-3532) and (2) a background replayer that uses sampled spans to auto-propose scheduler policy tweaks (ENG-3551).

title: Hybrid quant execution paths: FP8-aware kernels, INT8 dequant+matmul fusion and scheduler refactor
preview: Motivation: customers running large conversational workloads push two axes of optimization simultaneously: cheaper quantized kernels (INT8/FP8) and smarter scheduling to preserve latency SLOs when batching. This PR implements a hybrid execution strategy that (1) adds FP8-aware matmul kernels with per-channel scale metadata, (2) implements an INT8 dequant+matmul fusion execution path to avoid intermediate FP buffers when upstream weights are INT8 and downstream ops permit FP accumulation, and (3) refactors the request scheduler to expose an adaptive batch window and preemptive slotting to reduce head-of-line and tail latency during bursty traffic. Summary of changes: new kernel files under serving/runtime/kernels/{fp8_matmul.cc,fp8_matmul.h,quant_fusion.cc}, changes to kernel registry to prefer fused path when data layout and accumulation type are compatible, scheduler refactor in serving/runtime/scheduler/{adaptive_window.rs,slotting.rs} with a conservative default policy that preserves existing latency behavior, KV cache interaction updates in serving/runtime/cache to bypass copy-on-dequant for fused paths, continuous batching adjustments to prefer short windows for chat-like traffic and longer windows for bulk embed workloads, micro-bench harness and bench scripts added to infra/bench/quant_hybrid. Commits (high-level): 1) initial FP8 kernel implementation and microbench harness, 2) INT8 dequant+matmul fusion path and kernel registry integration, 3) scheduler refactor and policy configuration knobs, 4) KV cache bypass and tests, 5) docs + benchmarks + small followups. Files changed (high level): serving/runtime/kernels/* (new kernels and tests), serving/runtime/registry.cc (selection logic), serving/runtime/scheduler/* (refactor and policy), serving/runtime/cache/* (KV cache bypass), infra/bench/* (bench scripts), docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md (guidance and numbers), tests/unit/* (numerics and scheduler unit tests). Benchmarks: end-to-end microbench on a T4/80GB-equivalent collector shows FP8 matmul 1.8-2.1x faster than FP16 baseline for long sequences and 2.3-2.6x for short dense matmuls; INT8 fused path reduced memory pressure and improved throughput by ~1.4x in mixed workloads. Tail latency (p95-p99) improved by 10-20% across our chat-like workload mix when the adaptive batch window is enabled with default conservative params. Cost projection: for a typical chat endpoint these changes reduce per-token GPU cycles by ~18% at our sampled workload, with larger wins for model families that compress well to FP8. Testing and CI: added unit tests for numeric parity (relative error tracking with random seeds), integration tests for fused path selection, and scheduler unit tests with simulated arrival patterns. CI initially showed flakiness on scheduler timing tests due to coarse clocks in the test harness; tests stabilized after tightening timing expectations and adding deterministic seeds. Review conversation highlights: Liam O'Connor: "Numerical stability seems OK on the sampled seeds but please add a stress test with longer sequences and underflow/overflow envelopes." Author (Ava Chen): "Added stress test harness and long-sequence seeds, documented the worst-case relative error and safe fallbacks to FP16 accumulation in the registry when scale overflow is detected." Grace Patel: "Scheduler changes look good; can we make the adaptive window configurable per-route?" Author: "Yes, added per-route knobs with sane defaults and a global override for Dedicated customers." Rohit Mehra: "Bench numbers look promising; please add scripts to reproduce and commit summaries of raw results." Author: "Bench scripts committed to infra/bench with a README pointing to s3 artifact locations; raw CSVs attached to Linear ticket ENG-5821." CI & approvals: initial CI run failed on flaky timing test, second run passed; reviewers Grace and Rohit approved after perf/regression signoff; Liam gave an approve-after-changes tag once stress tests added. Migration and config: new config flags added to runtime config schema: quant.hybrid.enabled, quant.hybrid.strict_scale_check, scheduler.adaptive_window.enabled, scheduler.adaptive_window.max_ms. Default values preserve previous behavior unless quant.hybrid.enabled is set. Backwards compatibility: the fused path is opt-in via registry selection and falls back to legacy dequant path on numeric anomalies; no customer-facing API changes. Documentation: docs/perf/quant-hybrid.md contains recommendations on when to enable FP8/INT8 paths, how to tune scheduler knobs for chat vs embed workloads, and an appendix with reproduced bench numbers and command lines. Follow-ups: 1) larger FP8 coverage for other kernels (conv1d, rnn) tracked in ENG-5830, 2) investigate hardware-specific low-level kernels for A100/A800 in a follow-up PR, 3) expand regression testing to include stochastic load patterns in canary. Merge: squashed and merged to main on 2024-11-21 after approvals and green CI. Artifacts: bench outputs and CSVs uploaded to s3://redwood-ci/bench/pr-31207/.

title: Introduce soft-label probing and calibrated-expectation CI hook
preview:
Context and motivation: Some regressions manifest as shifts in the model's probability mass over semantically equivalent answers rather than hard label changes. Existing binary pass/fail gates can be too brittle (false positives) or miss subtle quality drift. This PR adds a 'soft-label prober' and a calibrated-expectation CI hook that (a) probes prompt cohorts with synthetic soft labels (probability distributions over acceptable outputs), (b) compares aggregate likelihood mass assigned to expected semantic clusters, and (c) uses a noise-aware historical model to set dynamic tolerances so teams get fewer spurious CI failures while retaining sensitivity to true regressions.

Goals: 1) detect semantic drift that doesn't appear as single-answer mismatches; 2) reduce noisy CI failures by modeling historical variance per-cohort; 3) provide clear remediation artifacts (example worst-case prompts, delta distributions, suggested rollback windows).

Implementation summary:
- Core: eval_harness.probers.soft_label_prober.py implements probing loop, clustering helper (semantic buckets via embed+kmeans), and soft-label matcher that accepts distribution-level comparisons (KL, Earth Mover Distance).
- CI hook: infra/ci/hooks/calibrated_expectation_hook.py integrates with existing eval CI runner, computes cohort-level scores, consults the historical-stats cache to derive an adaptive tolerance (percentile-based) and emits a structured failure payload to the CI dashboard.
- Persistence: add historical noise ledger in eval_harness/storage/noise_ledger.sqlite (migration + lightweight updater) to capture per-cohort variance over time.
- Tests: unit tests for matcher heuristics and an integration smoke test that runs a synthetic prompt cohort against a mocked model returning controlled probability vectors.
- Docs: docs/eval/soft_label_prober.md describing configuration knobs, recommended cohort sizing, and troubleshooting.

Checklist:
- [x] Core prober implementation
- [x] CI hook wiring and gating behavior
- [x] Historical noise model and migration path
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Basic docs and changelog entry
- [x] Add telemetry and tracing spans for debugging

What changed (high-level file list):
- eval_harness/probers/soft_label_prober.py (new)
- eval_harness/probers/utils/semantic_bucket.py (new)
- infra/ci/hooks/calibrated_expectation_hook.py (new)
- eval_harness/storage/noise_ledger.py (new)
- tests/probers/test_soft_label_prober.py (new)
- tests/ci/test_calibrated_hook_integration.py (new)
- docs/eval/soft_label_prober.md (new)
- README and CHANGELOG updates (small edits)

Review conversation (highlights):
- Marcus Patel (reviewer): "Nice design. Can you clarify how you avoid small-sample overfitting when the ledger has < 50 historical runs?"
- Ava Chen (author): "Good point — I added a fallback smoothing that blends global cohort priors with observed ledger variance (lines 110-130 of noise_ledger.py). For very small histories we expand the tolerance to the 90th percentile of global cohort variance and annotate the CI failure as low-confidence."
- Leah Gordon (reviewer): "Can we make the default metric KL->EMD? KL is asymmetric and spikes easily when supports diverge."
- Ava Chen: "Switched primary metric to Earth Mover Distance (EMD) and left KL as an optional diagnostic — updated tests and docs."
- Ravi Singh (reviewer): "Telemetry looks good; add one more tag for 'cohort_size' on the failure event."
- Ava Chen: "Added cohort_size and ledger_depth tags to the telemetry envelope (see infra/telemetry/span_kv usage)."

CI history and reruns:
- Initial CI run (linux/unit) failed due to a flaky deterministic-seed in the semantic_bucket clustering test; failure mode: non-deterministic kmeans seeding. Action: fixed by adding a seeded kmeans helper and a retry-with-alternate-seed strategy in tests.
- Second run surfaced a lint failure (import ordering). Fixed.
- Final run: all checks pass (unit/integration/lint/security static checks).

Observability and dashboards:
- Added a lightweight REST endpoint in infra/ci/reports to surface the last 30 calibrated failures and show per-cohort tolerance bands.
- Added Grafana row: eval.soft_label_prober.failures with a breakdown by cohort and EMD delta.

Operational notes and configuration:
- New config block (eval.prober.soft_label) supports: sample_size (default 200), bucket_count (default 8), tolerance_percentile (default 95), min_ledger_depth (default 50), fallback_global_percentile (default 90).
- Hook respects repo-level overrides via eval.yaml in repo root (see docs).
- When a hook fails it uploads: worst_n_prompts.json, cohort_delta_distribution.csv, and a small replay bundle to the CI artifact store.

Migration/compatibility:
- Migration script eval_harness/storage/migrations/0002_add_noise_ledger.py included and idempotent.
- Existing CI gates are not changed; this hook is opt-in per-branch via eval.yaml.

Performance:
- Prober runs a single pass with batched embedding calls (reuses SDK embeddings cache) and samples 200 prompts by default; measured CI runtime impact ~+18s for a typical prompt set (acceptable for nightly and pre-merge pipelines).

Security and privacy:
- No plaintext customer data is written to the ledger; prompts are hashed with salt from KMS per-organization and only aggregate statistics are stored.

Tests and determinism:
- Added deterministic fixtures and a seeded clustering helper to reduce test flakiness (addresses earlier failure).
- Integration test uses a mocked model that returns explicit probability vectors to validate EMD thresholds and failure payload contents.

Follow-ups (post-merge):
- Add an autosuggest in the hook that recommends candidate rollback windows (ENG-2205).
- Evaluate calibrating on per-model family basis rather than global cohort (ENG-2221).

text:
Context and motivation: Some regressions manifest as shifts in the model's probability mass over semantically equivalent answers rather than hard label changes. Existing binary pass/fail gates can be too brittle (false positives) or miss subtle quality drift. This PR adds a 'soft-label prober' and a calibrated-expectation CI hook that (a) probes prompt cohorts with synthetic soft labels (probability distributions over acceptable outputs), (b) compares aggregate likelihood mass assigned to expected semantic clusters, and (c) uses a noise-aware historical model to set dynamic tolerances so teams get fewer spurious CI failures while retaining sensitivity to true regressions.

Goals: 1) detect semantic drift that doesn't appear as single-answer mismatches; 2) reduce noisy CI failures by modeling historical variance per-cohort; 3) provide clear remediation artifacts (example worst-case prompts, delta distributions, suggested rollback windows).

Implementation summary:
- Core: eval_harness.probers.soft_label_prober.py implements probing loop, clustering helper (semantic buckets via embed+kmeans), and soft-label matcher that accepts distribution-level comparisons (KL, Earth Mover Distance).
- CI hook: infra/ci/hooks/calibrated_expectation_hook.py integrates with existing eval CI runner, computes cohort-level scores, consults the historical-stats cache to derive an adaptive tolerance (percentile-based) and emits a structured failure payload to the CI dashboard.
- Persistence: add historical noise ledger in eval_harness/storage/noise_ledger.sqlite (migration + lightweight updater) to capture per-cohort variance over time.
- Tests: unit tests for matcher heuristics and an integration smoke test that runs a synthetic prompt cohort against a mocked model returning controlled probability vectors.
- Docs: docs/eval/soft_label_prober.md describing configuration knobs, recommended cohort sizing, and troubleshooting.

Checklist:
- [x] Core prober implementation
- [x] CI hook wiring and gating behavior
- [x] Historical noise model and migration path
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Basic docs and changelog entry
- [x] Add telemetry and tracing spans for debugging

What changed (high-level file list):
- eval_harness/probers/soft_label_prober.py (new)
- eval_harness/probers/utils/semantic_bucket.py (new)
- infra/ci/hooks/calibrated_expectation_hook.py (new)
- eval_harness/storage/noise_ledger.py (new)
- tests/probers/test_soft_label_prober.py (new)
- tests/ci/test_calibrated_hook_integration.py (new)
- docs/eval/soft_label_prober.md (new)
- README and CHANGELOG updates (small edits)

Review conversation (highlights):
- Marcus Patel (reviewer): "Nice design. Can you clarify how you avoid small-sample overfitting when the ledger has < 50 historical runs?"
- Ava Chen (author): "Good point — I added a fallback smoothing that blends global cohort priors with observed ledger variance (lines 110-130 of noise_ledger.py). For very small histories we expand the tolerance to the 90th percentile of global cohort variance and annotate the CI failure as low-confidence."
- Leah Gordon (reviewer): "Can we make the default metric KL->EMD? KL is asymmetric and spikes easily when supports diverge."
- Ava Chen: "Switched primary metric to Earth Mover Distance (EMD) and left KL as an optional diagnostic — updated tests and docs."
- Ravi Singh (reviewer): "Telemetry looks good; add one more tag for 'cohort_size' on the failure event."
- Ava Chen: "Added cohort_size and ledger_depth tags to the telemetry envelope (see infra/telemetry/span_kv usage)."

CI history and reruns:
- Initial CI run (linux/unit) failed due to a flaky deterministic-seed in the semantic_bucket clustering test; failure mode: non-deterministic kmeans seeding. Action: fixed by adding a seeded kmeans helper and a retry-with-alternate-seed strategy in tests.
- Second run surfaced a lint failure (import ordering). Fixed.
- Final run: all checks pass (unit/integration/lint/security static checks).

Observability and dashboards:
- Added a lightweight REST endpoint in infra/ci/reports to surface the last 30 calibrated failures and show per-cohort tolerance bands.
- Added Grafana row: eval.soft_label_prober.failures with a breakdown by cohort and EMD delta.

Operational notes and configuration:
- New config block (eval.prober.soft_label) supports: sample_size (default 200), bucket_count (default 8), tolerance_percentile (default 95), min_ledger_depth (default 50), fallback_global_percentile (default 90).
- Hook respects repo-level overrides via eval.yaml in repo root (see docs).
- When a hook fails it uploads: worst_n_prompts.json, cohort_delta_distribution.csv, and a small replay bundle to the CI artifact store.

Migration/compatibility:
- Migration script eval_harness/storage/migrations/0002_add_noise_ledger.py included and idempotent.
- Existing CI gates are not changed; this hook is opt-in per-branch via eval.yaml.

Performance:
- Prober runs a single pass with batched embedding calls (reuses SDK embeddings cache) and samples 200 prompts by default; measured CI runtime impact ~+18s for a typical prompt set (acceptable for nightly and pre-merge pipelines).

Security and privacy:
- No plaintext customer data is written to the ledger; prompts are hashed with salt from KMS per-organization and only aggregate statistics are stored.

Tests and determinism:
- Added deterministic fixtures and a seeded clustering helper to reduce test flakiness (addresses earlier failure).
- Integration test uses a mocked model that returns explicit probability vectors to validate EMD thresholds and failure payload contents.

title: orchestrate-credential-fabric-and-async-generator-bridge
preview: Context: Multiple SDK teams requested a small, consistent surface for credential fallback and streaming ergonomics. Customers reported integration friction when migrating long-lived streaming consumers (chat/observable pipelines) between Python/TS/Go — token refresh timing, retry floor semantics, and typed async generator shapes diverged. This PR introduces the "credential fabric" concept: a lightweight orchestration layer that centralizes credential selection/fallback, prefetch 'stitch' mode, and a small compatibility shim that yields a unified async-generator/iterator surface across languages. Motivation: reduce app-side glue, enable shared retry presets, and make typed stream consumers portable across SDKs without breaking existing integrations. Summary of changes: - Introduces a credential-fabric layer in the monorepo that exports a language-agnostic contract for: preferred credential, fallback queue, prefetch/stitch hints, and heartbeat-aware TTLs. - Python SDK: add redwood.cred_fabric.CredentialOrchestrator (async) + typed AsyncGenerator helpers (py SDK v2.5.0). Improves __aiter__/aclose behavior so cancelation races are deterministic. - TypeScript SDK: add async-generator facade with typed envelope and a small pullstream helper that understands the fabric prefetch hints; bump TS SDK to v1.29.0. - Go SDK: introduce IteratorWithCtx type and a credential adapter that exposes the fabric's lease and heartbeat; go-sdk bumped to v1.9.0. - Retry semantics: introduce an adaptive-retry-floor preset (stabilizes retry backoff when credential switch occurs to avoid thundering reconnections). - Backcompat: provide a thin compatibility shim for legacy consumers (v1.x) that continue to use older iterator shapes; new default surface is opt-in via SDK config flag enable_cred_fabric. - Tests: cross-sdk integration tests that run a simulated token-rotation storm and validate latency/incident metrics. Migration notes: - To opt into the new unified stream surface, set SDK config enable_cred_fabric=true. - For Python users: replace manual token-refresh loops with CredentialOrchestrator.subscribe() AsyncGenerator. - For TS/Go: adapters mirror the Python flow; examples added under redwood/examples/streaming/cred-fabric. Performance and telemetry: On representative streaming workload (50k short streams), end-to-end median connect latency reduced by ~8% and recovered error-rate under token rotation improved 3x due to the adaptive-retry-floor. Rollout plan: feature flag gated, staged rollout to beta customers, SDK docs + migration guide included. CI/QA: unit tests added for all three SDKs, cross-sdk e2e run in matrix. See release_notes for user-facing summary.

title: streaming-reconstruction-guard-and-toolcall-stable-handback
preview: Motivation: We observed a class of in-production incidents where SSE fragment splits inside escaped JSON strings or multi-byte UTF-8 sequences could cause downstream consumers to see re-ordered function/tool call boundaries or emit malformed partial JSON. This PR introduces a lightweight runtime 'reconstruction guard' that assigns per-fragment fidelity credits and a deterministic handback protocol to ensure toolcall boundaries remain ordered across retransmit/replay and partial-frame repair. Summary of changes: 1) runtime/streaming: add FragmentFidelityLedger and a reconstruction guard that tracks credit windows and stalls handback until a safe boundary is observed or the ledger times out; 2) sse/parser: introduce escape-aware tail rewriter to avoid splitting inside escapes and normalize trailing delimiters before emission; 3) compat/openai: emit X-Redwood-Handback-Fidelity header and annotate stream events with handback_id so OpenAI-compat clients can prefer resumable handoff points; 4) protocol: deterministic toolcall handoff algorithm that converts ephemeral function-call tokens into ordered handback checkpoints to prevent interleaving when fragments arrive out-of-order; 5) tests: large suite of synthetic fragment replays and property tests that simulate jittered, coalesced, and reordered chunks. Implementation notes: The reconstruction guard uses a small in-memory ledger per connection (bounded to 32 entries) and a sliding credit window to avoid unbounded buffering. When ledger credit is insufficient, the connection flushes an escape-normalized delimiter and advances a handback checkpoint; this behavior preserves low-latency in common cases while preventing rare misorders. The OpenAI-compat annotations are backward compatible: older clients ignore handback_id and continue to operate normally. Checklist: - [x] Core runtime changes implemented and covered by unit tests - [x] End-to-end tests with python and ts SDKs - [x] Backwards compatibility validated against existing OpenAI-compat integration tests - [x] Performance microbenchmarks show <0.6% median latency regression on p4d for synthetic workloads - [x] Feature flagged behind runtime.feature.streaming_reconstruct_guard rollout plan: feature off in production initial cut; enable for 10% traffic with canary instrumentation and latency/error dashboards; expand to 100% after 48h with no regressions. Related: ENG-2407 describes incident notes and replay corpus. This description includes small illustrative pseudo-patch: "if (fragment.endsWith('\\') || insideEscape(fragment)) normalizeEscapeTail(fragment)" which is implemented in sse/parser/tail_rewrite.go.

title: Add cohort-aware spiral prober and rollback curator for transient regression isolation
preview: Motivation: We repeatedly see short-lived latency spikes that trigger standard canaries and cause unnecessary rollbacks. This PR implements a cohort-aware probing strategy ("spiral prober") plus a lightweight rollback curator that isolates transient regressions from persistent degradations and provides a staged rollback budget for safe cutovers. The change is intended to reduce noise-driven rollbacks while preserving sensitivity to real regressions.

title: hackathon: add internal LLM routing prototype
preview:
Motivation: for the internal hackathon we built a small routing microservice that demonstrates routing inference requests across two lightweight model variants (fast/cheap vs accurate/slow) based on simple latency and cost heuristics. This is a demo/prototype only and intended to seed a conversation about smarter routing policies that could later be integrated into the runtime.

What this PR does: adds a toy HTTP microservice (Python/Flask) that accepts Redwood-style request payloads, applies a configurable policy to choose a target model variant, forwards the request to a mock backend adapter, and returns annotated response metadata. Includes a README with run instructions, a small policy DSL (yaml) and unit tests for policy evaluation.

Key design notes: the service is intentionally minimal: policy evaluation is synchronous and in-process, there is no persistence, and backends are mocked by default. The routing policy supports rules based on max_latency_ms, cost_threshold, and explicit model_tags. This is NOT production-ready: no auth, no rate limiting, limited error handling.

How to run locally: python -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt && FLASK_ENV=development FLASK_APP=app.py flask run --port 5003. See README for example curl payloads and expected annotated response.

Checklist: [x] prototype service and tests, [x] README and example payloads, [x] CI runs unit tests, [ ] integration w/real runtime (future).

Risks and follow-ups: this PR intentionally keeps dependencies tiny. If we iterate on this concept for product work we should extract policy evaluation into a library, add async evaluation to avoid blocking the request path, add metrics and tracing, and implement authentication/authorization and rate limiting.

Notes for reviewers: focus on clarity of policy DSL and test coverage rather than micro-optimizations. The implementation aims to be pedagogical for hackathon demos.

title: Introduce adaptive pager suppression using noise profiling
preview:
Motivation: paging fatigue from repetitive low-value alerts continues to erode oncall bandwidth. This change adds an adaptive pager-suppression subsystem to incident-bot that builds and uses per-service noise profiles to decide when to suppress, group, or throttle notifications. Key goals: reduce noisy low-action pages by ~40% in a test cohort, provide clear feedback to oncall (why a page was suppressed), and make suppression policies configurable and auditable.

What this PR does: 
- Adds a lightweight noise profiler that accumulates sketch summaries of alert fingerprints (hash, signature dimensions) and computes short-window noise scores (noise_rate, dedupe_entropy, throttle_suggestion).
- Implements an adaptive suppression policy engine that can: suppress (no page), group (single consolidated page for an incident burst), or debounce (delay page by Xs) based on policy rules and the noise score. Policies are evaluated per-service and fall back to org defaults.
- Adds a user feedback loop: suppressed pages include a one-click feedback link which updates the profiling signals (helpful/not-helpful) and is exposed to SRE dashboards.
- Integrates the profiler with slo-toolkit so teams can import noise budgets and weight suppression aggressiveness by SLO risk.
- Exposes runtime metrics and trace hooks to aid debugging and post-incident analysis.
- CLI flags and runtime config documented for ops teams, with safe opt-out (disable suppression per service).

Checklist: 
- [x] Design doc linked in PR (Linear ENG-2914)
- [x] Unit tests for policy engine and profiler aggregation
- [x] e2e smoke test exercising suppression path + feedback loop
- [x] Metrics (Prometheus) and tracing instrumentation added
- [x] Docs: incident-bot README + onboarding note in docs/incidents/

Notes: the algorithm deliberately uses approximate sketches to bound memory and keep profiler replica-safe. This PR is conservative about default aggressiveness and ships with a 30-day telemetry-only mode before auto-suppression is engaged (configurable).

Related links: Linear ENG-2914, RFC: /docs/rfcs/2024/noise-profiling.md, internal dashboard: /dashboards/ops/noise-profile-summary

Release notes: see release_notes field.

title: Safe Rejoin Coordinator with Connection Epoch Fence
preview:
Motivation: During a cross-region incident we observed a small class of degradations where connections that rejoined quickly after transient network flaps caused KV cache overwrite and high tail latency spikes. This PR introduces a Safe Rejoin Coordinator and a Connection Epoch Fence to ensure rejoining nodes validate epoch affinity before accepting requests that may mutate or repopulate per-connection KV cache entries. The change reduces risk of stale-kv stomping and provides a short quiesce window for region rejoin sequences.

Scope: single runtime-side coordinator service + lightweight epoch fence protocol and control-plane signals for coordinated rejoin. The implementation intentionally keeps the runtime fast-path unchanged for steady state; the coordinator only participates during node state transitions.

What this PR changes: 1) runtime/rejoin/coordinator: new coordinator that tracks node epochs and issues timeboxed rejoin permits; 2) runtime/kv/fence: epoch-checks on KV-preserve paths to reject/forward ambiguous rejoin traffic; 3) controlplane/handlers: small handshake to annotate node reprovision events with epoch tokens; 4) ops tooling: a console toggle to enable conservative quiesce windows during rollouts.

Testing and validation: extensive unit tests for epoch validation logic, integration test that simulates multi-region reconnects with packet loss and ensures no KV overwrite, and a perf soak showing <1% p50 impact in steady-state. CI includes a canary job that runs the rejoin scenario harness.

Rollout plan: feature-gate off by default on main; staged rollout to production via the console toggle. Initial rollout will target non-critical regions (eu-west-2, ap-south-1) for 48 hours, then gradual expansion with telemetry checks for KV-inconsistency metrics. Runbook included for emergency rollback.

Checklist: [x] Unit tests added; [x] Integration scenario added to perf lab; [x] Console toggle implemented; [x] Release notes drafted.

Notes: This is designed as a low-risk path to reduce a very specific class of tail latency incidents tied to rejoin races. It is not intended to replace longer-term topology upgrades but mitigates immediate customer impact and enables safer hot restarts across regions.

title: Introduce hermetic Go CI runtime and slimline pipeline shim
preview: Motivation: our existing CI relies on many small shell scripts invoked from workflow YAMLs; those scripts are a common source of subtle failures (environment differences, word-splitting bugs, silent pipefail issues) and make local dev/CI parity hard. This change introduces a small hermetic Go-based CI runtime (ci-runner) that replaces the most fragile shell entrypoints and consolidates common plumbing (workspace setup, action inputs normalization, token-safe logging, artifact staging). Goals: (1) reduce flakes by making CI entrypoints deterministic, (2) shrink workflow YAMLs to declarative stages and let the runner handle retries/backoff, (3) centralize action pinning and dependency-check hooks, and (4) pave way for incremental migration of legacy jobs. Work in this PR: - Add tools/ci/runner/main.go (single binary ~7 file packages) that implements: workspace bootstrap, env validation, action pin checks, optional sandboxing flags, and a small plugin interface for short-lived job steps. - Replace top-level shell scripts used by heavy integration lanes with thin shims that invoke ci-runner (scripts retained for compatibility during rollout). - Update .github/workflows/ci.yml to call the runner with structured inputs and reduce ad-hoc step logic. - Introduce tools/ci/runner/go.mod and minimal Dockerfile for hermetic images used by self-hosted runners. - Pin a handful of GitHub Actions SHAs and add action-compatibility guard that logs warning when an action input signature changes. - Add a lightweight dep-health check that warns (non-blocking) for transitive devdeps introduced by dependency bumps. Rollout plan: 1) Merge to main behind a feature flag (CI flag: USE_HERMETIC_RUNNER=true) so all jobs continue to run in parallel with legacy shells for two cycles. 2) Monitor flaky-test rate, CI median runtime, and failed step entropy for 3 consecutive releases. 3) Flip default flag, keep legacy shims for 30 days as a hotfix rollback path. 4) Remove legacy scripts after telemetry validates stability. Checklist: - [x] add go runner source and minimal tests - [x] update CI workflows to call runner - [x] pin key GH Actions SHAs - [x] add documentation in tools/ci/runner/README.md - [x] smoke test on release branch - [x] add linear tickets for follow-ups (ENG-4872, ENG-4924) Related work: smaller follow-ups will move lint and release signing steps into the runner as plugins (see ENG-4901). text: Motivation: our existing CI relies on many small shell scripts invoked from workflow YAMLs; those scripts are a common source of subtle failures (environment differences, word-splitting bugs, silent pipefail issues) and make local dev/CI parity hard. This change introduces a small hermetic Go-based CI runtime (ci-runner) that replaces the most fragile shell entrypoints and consolidates common plumbing (workspace setup, action inputs normalization, token-safe logging, artifact staging). Goals: (1) reduce flakes by making CI entrypoints deterministic, (2) shrink workflow YAMLs to declarative stages and let the runner handle retries/backoff, (3) centralize action pinning and dependency-check hooks, and (4) pave way for incremental migration of legacy jobs. Work in this PR: - Add tools/ci/runner/main.go (single binary ~7 file packages) that implements: workspace bootstrap, env validation, action pin checks, optional sandboxing flags, and a small plugin interface for short-lived job steps. - Replace top-level shell scripts used by heavy integration lanes with thin shims that invoke ci-runner (scripts retained for compatibility during rollout). - Update .github/workflows/ci.yml to call the runner with structured inputs and reduce ad-hoc step logic. - Introduce tools/ci/runner/go.mod and minimal Dockerfile for hermetic images used by self-hosted runners. - Pin a handful of GitHub Actions SHAs and add action-compatibility guard that logs warning when an action input signature changes. - Add a lightweight dep-health check that warns (non-blocking) for transitive devdeps introduced by dependency bumps. Rollout plan: 1) Merge to main behind a feature flag (CI flag: USE_HERMETIC_RUNNER=true) so all jobs continue to run in parallel with legacy shells for two cycles. 2) Monitor flaky-test rate, CI median runtime, and failed step entropy for 3 consecutive releases. 3) Flip default flag, keep legacy shims for 30 days as a hotfix rollback path. 4) Remove legacy scripts after telemetry validates stability. title: model-family kernel picker + GQA/MQA tuning + Qwen KV-cache stride fix
preview: Context: we observed higher tail latency and sporadic OOMs when serving mixed workloads containing Mistral, Qwen and Llama family models. Root causes were: (1) suboptimal kernel selection for fused rotary/attention on Mistral and some Llama variants, (2) misaligned group-quantization (GQA) code paths that spilled to host under certain MQA layouts, and (3) a KV-cache pointer/stride bug in Qwen cache layout that created fragmentation under continuous batching. This PR introduces a model-family aware kernel picker (heuristics based on model_family, hidden_size, seq_dist) that prefers fused-rotary + threaded attention kernels for Mistral/Llama; adds quantization-aware attention execution paths to handle GQA/MQA edge cases (avoids fp16->host spills by switching to a compact packed layout when group sizes mismatch); fixes Qwen KV-cache stride/pointer arithmetic and compacts cache layout to reduce working set; and adjusts continuous-batcher thresholds and micro-batch sizing per-model to prevent cross-model OOMs. Also includes unit/integration tests, perf microbench harness updates, and a staged rollout plan (canary -> 30% -> global). Motivation and impact: these are production-facing fixes that improve latency and stability without requiring model rewrites. Backwards-compatible toggles are exposed as runtime knobs (kernel_picker.mode, quant.tune_mode, kvcache.compact_layout) that default to safe behavior. Rollout notes: start with canary in us-west-2 for 48 hours, monitor token-level latency and OOM counts, then 30% for 72 hours before global.

title: introduce-cost-sentinel-prefetch-catalog-and-quant-tier-optimizer
preview: Context: Customers with mixed bursty and steady traffic were seeing suboptimal unit economics from 'eager' prefetch defaults and a single global quantization policy. This change introduces a cost-aware prefetch coalescer (Cost Sentinel) that builds a lightweight tenant prefetch catalog and a Quant Tier Optimizer that selects conservative quant profiles at rollout time. Motivation: reduce per-token cost on mid-size tenants while preserving latency SLOs and preventing quality regressions during quant rollouts. Key changes: 1) server/prefetch/cost_sentinel.py — new coalescer that synthesizes tenant prefetch catalogs from short-window request traces, implements an eviction heuristic (LRU by last-use + size score) and exposes a bounded catalog API; 2) service/econ/quant_selector.go — runtime policy to map tenant catalogs + workload shape to a quant tier (fp16 / int8-safe / int8-aggressive) with safety gates based on eval-set regressions and token loss thresholds; 3) cli/econ-inspect/main.go — developer CLI to inspect tenant prefetch catalog, simulate cost delta for candidate quant tiers, and produce a dry-run report; 4) docs/operator/prefetch.md — operational guidance on feature flagging, rollout windows, and recommended observation queries; 5) tests/e2e/prefetch_cost_test.py and unit tests for quant stability. Rollout plan: feature flag default off; enable on a small cohort (10 tenants) with shadow-prefetch mode for 48h, then canary with 1% traffic shift; automatic rollback if token-loss delta > 0.6% or latency P99 increases by >10ms. Related: closes ENG-3456 (prefetch defaults), references SUP-4123 (customer incident with cold-prefetch thrash).

title: Introduce quiet-merge signal and lint telemetry adapter
preview: Context: our presubmit lanes produce noisy merge traffic and noisy alerting for non-actionable lint regressions. This PR introduces two complementary pieces: 1) a lightweight 'quiet-merge' signal that lets the merge service suppress non-critical notifications and avoid triggering heavyweight post-merge jobs on low-risk infra/docs-only changes; and 2) a lint telemetry adapter that collects anonymized lint rule violation counts and time-to-fix metrics for presubmit failures so we can prioritize rule maintenance and reduce flakiness. Motivation: reduce pager noise and CI cost from churny lint failures, enable data-driven lint rule triage, and create a simple opt-in signal for release automation to skip non-essential post-merge jobs. Work included: - new CI input flag and API hook consumed by merge-orchestrator - presubmit hook that annotates PR metadata with quiet-merge eligibility reasons - telemetry adapter that emits compact counters to ci-telemetry (rule_id, severity, presubmit_stage) - docs and SDK shim for internal tooling to opt into quiet merges Checklist: - [x] implement quiet-merge signal and merge-orchestrator support - [x] add lint telemetry adapter and batching exporter - [x] unit tests for hook and adapter - [x] integration test in staging CI lane - [x] docs (console+infra runbook) - [x] add Linear links and update monitoring runbook Commits (summary): 1) add quiet-merge input and merge-orchestrator flag (3 commits) 2) implement presubmit hook and eligibility evaluator (2 commits) 3) add lint-telemetry adapter + batching exporter (2 commits) 4) tests and mocks (3 commits) 5) docs, runbook, changelog (1 commit) Changed areas (high-level): - ci/merge-orchestrator/src/main.rs: add quiet_merge handling and eval gate - ci/presubmit-hooks/lint_eligibility.py: new eligibility evaluator used by presubmit - infra/ci-templates/merge-job.yaml: conditional skip when quiet_merge=true - infra/telemetry/telemetry-adapters/lint_adapter.go: collects per-rule counters and ships batched events - sdk/internal/ci/README.md: usage and opt-in instructions - tests/integration/quiet_merge_end_to_end_test.sh: staging smoke test Illustrative diffs (small snippets):
